preamble.summary: Belshazzar's feast and the writing on the wall. The OG has notable differences from the MT/Theodotion: it identifies the queen differently, has variant details about the feast, and in some witnesses expands the backstory. Theodotion follows the MT closely.
preamble.notable_variants: The OG expands the queen's speech, adds details about the vessels from the temple, and varies in its account of who interpreted the writing. The four Aramaic words (MENE MENE TEKEL UPHARSIN) are transliterated differently in the two Greek versions.
preamble.structural_notes: All three versions have 31 verses (MT numbering: 30 in some systems). The OG's verse lengths vary from the MT.

variant_notes: No significant variant between the LXX/Theodotion and the MT for this verse.

The OG adds detail about which gods were praised — specifying idols of gold, silver, bronze, iron, wood, and stone — expanding the MT's briefer notice.

verses.mt_rendering: the fingers of a man's hand
variant_notes: Both Greek versions preserve the eerie image. No significant difference.
OG uses morphē (form/appearance); Theodotion uses the same idiom as the MT.
No significant variant between the LXX/Theodotion and the MT for this verse.
No significant variant between the LXX/Theodotion and the MT for this verse.
No significant variant between the LXX/Theodotion and the MT for this verse.
The identity of 'the queen' is debated — likely the queen mother (Nitocris or Nebuchadnezzar's widow), not Belshazzar's wife. The OG adds clarifying language suggesting it is the queen mother.

verses.lxx_reading: OG: πνεῦμα θεοῦ / Th: πνεῦμα θεοῦ ἅγιον
verses.variant_rendering: OG: 'spirit of God' / Th: 'spirit of the holy God'
verses.mt_rendering: the spirit of the holy gods
variant_notes: The Aramaic elahin qaddishin is plural ('holy gods'). Theodotion makes this singular and adds 'holy' as an adjective of God rather than gods — a monotheistic theological adjustment.

verses.lxx_reading: OG: Μανη θεκελ φαρες / Th: Μανε θεκελ φαρες
variant_notes: The Greek transliterations differ from the Aramaic. Notably, both Greek versions have only one MENE (not doubled as in MT). UPHARSIN becomes PHARES (the plural is dropped). These differences may reflect a different Vorlage or interpretive smoothing.

verses.lxx_reading: Μανη — ἐμέτρησεν ὁ θεὸς τὴν βασιλείαν σου
verses.variant_rendering: Mane — God has measured your kingdom
verses.mt_rendering: MENE — God has numbered your kingdom
variant_notes: OG uses emetrēsen (measured); Theodotion ērithmēsen (numbered). The MT's m-n-h root means 'to count/number.'

verses.mt_rendering: TEKEL — you have been weighed in the balances
variant_notes: Both Greek versions agree with the MT. The weighing-and-found-wanting image is identical.
The Aramaic p-r-s wordplay (PERES/Persians) is lost in Greek. Both versions render the meaning directly.
No significant variant between the LXX/Theodotion and the MT for this verse.
No significant variant between the LXX/Theodotion and the MT for this verse.

verses.lxx_reading: OG: Δαρεῖος ὁ Μῆδος / Th adds: Δαρεῖος ὁ Μῆδος
variant_notes: Both Greek versions retain 'Darius the Mede,' whose historical identification remains debated. The OG adds that he was 'son of Astyages' — a detail absent from the MT and Theodotion.
